title: Making Plans with Friends
situation: It's Thursday evening and you're trying to make weekend plans with your friend group chat.

phrases.hindi: yaar weekend pe kuch karte hain?
phrases.english: Dude shall we do something this weekend?
phrases.context: 'kuch karte hain' = let's do something — casual suggestion

phrases.hindi: agar Saturday free ho toh chalein kahin?
phrases.english: If you're free Saturday then shall we go somewhere?

phrases.hindi: main toh aa jaunga, tum batao
phrases.english: I'll definitely come, you guys say
phrases.context: 'toh' here = definitely/for sure. 'tum batao' = you decide

phrases.hindi: Connaught Place chalein? wahan naya restaurant khula hai
phrases.english: Shall we go to CP? A new restaurant opened there

phrases.hindi: done, 7 baje milte hain metro pe
phrases.english: Done, let's meet at 7 at the metro

phrases.hindi: late mat hona, pichli baar ek ghanta wait kiya tha
phrases.english: Don't be late, last time I waited one hour
phrases.context: Friendly warning — totally normal among friends

grammar_notes: Future tense: verb root + unga/ega/ogi/enge etc. 'Main jaunga' = I will go (male). 'Main jaungi' = I will go (female)
'Chalein?' = shall we go? — the 'ein' ending makes it a suggestion for 'we'
'Baje' = o'clock. 'Saat baje' = at 7. 'Kitne baje?' = what time?
culture_notes: Plans in India are fluid — 'pakka' (confirmed) vs 'dekhte hain' (let's see = probably no)
Being 15-30 min late is culturally normal among friends — but don't admit that to anyone

skill_breakdown.explanation: Verb root + suffixes based on gender/person. Male 'I': -unga (jaunga). Female 'I': -ungi (jaungi). 'You/He': -ega (jayega). 'She': -egi (jayegi). 'We/They/Respect': -enge (jayenge).
'Agar' = if, 'toh' = then. Structure: agar + condition + toh + result. 'Toh' is often dropped in casual speech. You can also start with the condition and just add 'toh'.

more_examples.english: if you have time then let me know
if you feel like it then come over

skill_breakdown.explanation: 'Chalein?' (shall we go?), 'karte hain' (let's do it), 'aa jao' (come over), 'chalo' (let's go/come on). These are the building blocks of all plans.

more_examples.hindi: ghar pe aao, kuch khate hain

more_examples.english: come on dude, it'll be fine

practice_prompt: It's Friday. You want to go to a new cafe in Hauz Khas Village this Saturday with friends. One friend is saying 'dekhte hain' (non-committal). Try to make a firm plan — suggest a time, a meeting point, and convince the reluctant friend.
